schools.summary: Gill: Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, either with his own ears, overhearing their discourse in their tents or in the field, or from the report of others, his wives or some of his friends who thought proper to acquaint him with it; Keil and Delitzsch note the envy of Laban's sons at Jacob's increase; John recording (in the narrative) Jacob hearing Laban's sons complain that he had taken away all that was their father's and gotten his wealth — the envious murmuring that warned Jacob his welcome was worn out (Jacob, now greatly increased, hearing 'the words of Laban's sons' murmuring against him: 'Jacob hath taken away all that was our father's; and of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory'; the spirit of the complaint, that it is the voice of envy and covetousness, the sons of Laban grudging Jacob's God-given prosperity and falsely charging him with having stripped their father, when in truth his wealth was the just reward of his labour and God's blessing, this murmuring being the first sign to Jacob that his time in Padan-aram was ending, the household turning against him, so the envy of Laban's sons becomes the providential occasion warning Jacob to look homeward).

themes: and he heard the words of Laban's sons
Jacob hath taken away all that was our father's
of that which was our father's hath he gotten all this glory
the envy of Laban's sons warning Jacob homeward

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob observed Laban's looks, that he might gather from thence how he took his prosperity, what were his thoughts about it, and what he might expect from him on that account; Keil and Delitzsch note the change in Laban's bearing toward Jacob; John recording Jacob beholding Laban's countenance, that it was not toward him as before — the souring of Laban's face betraying his changed heart (besides the sons' murmuring, Jacob also 'beheld the countenance of Laban, and, behold, it was not toward him as before'; the reading of the face, that Jacob, watchful and discerning, marked that Laban's looks had soured, the old friendliness gone, and from this changed countenance gathered Laban's inward grudging and ill-will at his prosperity; the face often betraying the heart, and Laban's altered look being a plain sign that envy had turned him against his son-in-law, so both the words of the sons and the face of the father combined to warn Jacob that he was no longer welcome and must think of departing).

themes: and Jacob beheld the countenance of Laban
it was not toward him as before
the souring of Laban's face betraying his heart
the changed countenance warning Jacob to depart

schools.summary: Gill: this was said in answer to a prayer of Jacob's, or, seeing what difficulties and discouragements Jacob laboured under, the Lord appeared unto him for his encouragement and instruction how to proceed; Adam Clarke: the LORD said, Return, and I will be with thee — the divine command joined to the gracious promise of God's accompanying presence; the LORD commanding Jacob to return to the land of his fathers and his kindred, with the promise 'I will be with thee' — the divine call homeward sealed with the promise of God's presence (amid the envy of the household, 'the LORD said unto Jacob, Return unto the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred'; the timeliness and grace of this word, that it comes just when Laban and his sons have turned against Jacob, God himself now directing his steps homeward to Canaan, the land of promise and of his fathers, and the command sealed with the sweetest promise, 'and I will be with thee'; the same God who calls also engaging to accompany, Jacob not sent back alone to face Esau and the perils of the way but with the assurance of God's own presence, the all-sufficient comfort and security for the journey, so the believer's path of obedience is lit by the promise of God's abiding presence).

themes: and the LORD said unto Jacob, Return unto the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred
and I will be with thee
the divine call homeward sealed with the promise of presence
the God who commands engaging to accompany

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob, having this encouragement and direction from the Lord, which seems to have been given him in the field while he was attending his flocks, dispatched a messenger home to his wives, one of his servants or under shepherds (the Targum of Jonathan says it was his son Naphtali); John recording Jacob sending to call Rachel and Leah out to the field to his flock — the prudent summoning of his wives to a private place to lay the matter before them (Jacob, having received God's command, taking a wise and considerate step: 'Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field unto his flock'; the prudence and courtesy of this, that Jacob does not act alone or high-handedly but sends for his wives to consult them and win their consent before so great a move, and calls them 'to the field,' away from Laban's house and listening ears, where among his flocks he could speak freely and secretly without fear of being overheard or hindered; the order of his household, that Jacob, though sure of God's call, deals tenderly and openly with Rachel and Leah, treating them as partners in the decision, so the verse shows Jacob's wisdom and his honourable dealing with his wives).

themes: and Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field unto his flock
calling his wives to a private place to confer
laying God's command before them to win their consent
Jacob's prudent and honourable dealing with his wives

schools.summary: Calvin: this address consists of two parts, for first Jacob speaks of his own integrity and expostulates concerning the wrong done him, then he ascribes all his prosperity to God; Gill notes Jacob says he sees their father's countenance is not toward him as before, taking no notice of what the sons had said; John recording Jacob telling his wives that their father's face was changed toward him, but the God of his father had been with him — the contrast between Laban's coldness and God's steady faithfulness (Jacob opening his case to Rachel and Leah by setting two things side by side: 'I see your father's countenance, that it is not toward me as before,' Laban's friendship turned to coldness and grudging, 'but the God of my father hath been with me'; the comfort of the contrast, that over against the changed and unreliable face of man stands the unchanging faithfulness of God, for though Laban had turned against him 'the God of my father,' the covenant God of Abraham and Isaac, had been constantly 'with' Jacob, blessing, prospering, and protecting him, Jacob resting not on Laban's favour now withdrawn but on God's faithful presence which never failed, so Jacob teaches his wives, and us, to look past the fickleness of men to the steadfast God).

themes: I see your father's countenance, that it is not toward me as before
but the God of my father hath been with me
the contrast of Laban's coldness and God's faithfulness
looking past the fickleness of men to the steadfast God

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob served Laban with all faithfulness and uprightness, with all diligence and industry, with all wisdom and prudence, with all his might and main, contriving the best methods and sparing no pains by day or night to take care of his flocks; Keil and Delitzsch note Jacob's appeal to his faithful service; John recording Jacob appealing to his wives that with all his power he had served their father — Jacob's appeal to his own diligent, wholehearted service as witness to his integrity (Jacob calling his wives to witness the manner of his service: 'ye know that with all my power I have served your father'; the force of the appeal, that Rachel and Leah themselves knew, better than any, how Jacob had laboured, 'with all my power,' with all faithfulness, diligence, skill, and unsparing effort, by day and night, keeping Laban's flocks; Jacob's conscience being clear, so that he can confidently call his own wives, Laban's daughters, to testify to his uprightness and the wholehearted service he had rendered their father through the long years, so Jacob's integrity is established by the appeal to those who knew him best, the contrast with Laban's deceit made the sharper).

themes: and ye know that with all my power I have served your father
Jacob's appeal to his wholehearted, diligent service
his clear conscience calling those who knew him best to witness
integrity established before the contrast with Laban's deceit

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Laban deceived Jacob and changed his wages ten times, but God suffered him not to hurt him; Gill: Laban deceived Jacob in the bargain he had made with him about his wages for keeping his cattle the six years past, after the fourteen years' servitude were ended; John recording Jacob saying that Laban had deceived him and changed his wages ten times, but God suffered him not to hurt him — Laban's persistent fraud overruled by God's protecting hand (Jacob setting Laban's treachery against God's faithfulness: 'your father hath deceived me, and changed my wages ten times; but God suffered him not to hurt me'; the two sides of the matter, on the one side Laban's repeated and shameless fraud, who 'deceived' Jacob and 'changed his wages ten times,' often, again and again, as often as Jacob's prudent bargain began to prosper him Laban altering the terms to cheat him, and on the other side the overruling providence of God, 'but God suffered him not to hurt me'; the comfort of this, that however Laban schemed and changed the bargain to Jacob's loss, God would not permit his servant to be really harmed but turned every fresh fraud to Jacob's gain, so the malice of men is bounded and overruled by the protecting God, who suffers them not to hurt his own).

themes: and your father hath deceived me, and changed my wages ten times
but God suffered him not to hurt me
Laban's repeated fraud overruled by God's protection
the malice of men bounded by the protecting God

schools.summary: Gill: sometimes Laban would say to Jacob, only the speckled lambs which the ewes shall bring forth shall be thine hire and not the spotted, or the ringstraked, or the brown, which according to the bargain should have been his; yet God ordered the flocks accordingly; John recording Jacob recounting how, whenever Laban named the speckled or the ringstraked as his wages, the cattle bore accordingly — the wonder of God overruling the very births of the flock to defeat Laban's greed (Jacob showing by example how God thwarted Laban's every change: 'if he said thus, The speckled shall be thy wages; then all the cattle bare speckled: and if he said thus, The ringstraked shall be thy hire; then bare all the cattle ringstraked'; the marvel of providence, that as often as Laban shifted the terms, naming whatever kind he thought would yield Jacob least, God so ordered the breeding of the flock that the cattle bore that very kind in abundance, turning each crafty change to Jacob's profit; this being no chance but the hand of God secretly governing nature itself to keep his promise to Jacob and to confound the greed of Laban, so the very wombs of the flock obeyed God against the schemes of the deceiver).

themes: if he said, The speckled shall be thy wages; then all the cattle bare speckled
if he said, The ringstraked shall be thy hire; then bare all the cattle ringstraked
God overruling the very births of the flock
the hand of God governing nature to defeat Laban's greed

schools.summary: John Wesley: God hath taken away the cattle of your father and given them to me; Gill notes not all of them, but a great part — Laban's flock was much lessened by these means, and more were taken away and came to Jacob's share than if Laban had abode by the original agreement; John recording Jacob summing it up, that God had taken away the cattle of their father and given them to him — the whole increase ascribed not to craft but to the gift of God (Jacob drawing the sum of the matter, giving God all the glory: 'thus God hath taken away the cattle of your father, and given them to me'; the heart of Jacob's confession, that he does not boast in his own cunning with the rods and the flocks but ascribes the whole transfer of wealth to God, it being God who 'taken away' a great part of Laban's cattle and 'given them' to Jacob, the rightful reward withheld by Laban's fraud now bestowed by God's justice; Jacob, taught by the dream that follows, seeing the hand of God, not his own devices, as the true cause of his prosperity, so Jacob crowns God as the giver of all his increase, a lesson for every believer to own God's hand in his blessings).

themes: thus God hath taken away the cattle of your father, and given them to me
Jacob ascribing his increase wholly to God
not boasting in his craft but owning God's gift
God the true giver of his prosperity

schools.summary: Gill: this was at the time that the cattle conceived (whether in spring or autumn cannot be said, for it seems this was twice a year), probably at the beginning of the six years' servitude or just before the agreement was made between Laban and Jacob, and was an instruction to Jacob; Adam Clarke notes the grisled (Hebrew beruddim, from barad, hail) as marked like hail-spots; John recording Jacob recounting the dream in which he saw the rams that leaped upon the cattle were ringstraked, speckled, and grisled — the God-given dream revealing that the increase was God's doing, not Jacob's device (Jacob relating to his wives the dream by which God had instructed and assured him: 'at the time that the cattle conceived... I saw in a dream, and, behold, the rams which leaped upon the cattle were ringstraked, speckled, and grisled'; the significance of the dream, that it showed Jacob that the marked offspring, which became his wages, came not chiefly from his own contrivance with the peeled rods but from God's ordering of the very breeding of the flock, the rams themselves in the vision being of the marked kinds; God thus teaching Jacob, and now teaching his wives, that the increase was a divine work given by God's hand, so the dream lifts Jacob's eyes from his own devices to the secret, sovereign working of God in his prosperity).

themes: I lifted up mine eyes, and saw in a dream
the rams which leaped upon the cattle were ringstraked, speckled, and grisled
the God-given dream revealing God's hand in the increase
Jacob's prosperity from God's ordering, not his own device

schools.summary: Gill: this was not a created angel, as Gabriel or any other, but the eternal one, the Son of God, who is afterwards called the God of Bethel and the Lord himself; John recording Jacob recounting that the Angel of God spake to him in the dream, calling 'Jacob,' and he answered 'Here am I' — the divine Angel addressing Jacob, identified by many as the Son of God (Jacob relating how the dream was no mere vision of rams but a divine encounter: 'the angel of God spake unto me in a dream, saying, Jacob: And I said, Here am I'; the weighty identity of this 'angel of God,' for he goes on to say 'I am the God of Bethel,' so that this is no created angel but, as Gill and many hold, the uncreated Angel, the Son of God, the LORD himself appearing in the form of an angel; the personal call 'Jacob' and Jacob's ready answer 'Here am I,' the response of an attentive, willing servant to his God, so the dream is revealed as a true theophany, God himself speaking to Jacob to direct and assure him).

debates.question: Who is the 'angel of God' who spoke to Jacob in the dream?

sides.position: Not a created angel but the uncreated Angel, the Son of God, the LORD himself — for he says 'I am the God of Bethel'

themes: and the angel of God spake unto me in a dream, saying, Jacob
And I said, Here am I
the divine Angel addressing Jacob
held by many to be the Son of God himself

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'grisled,' Hebrew beruddim, from barad (hail), means marked as with hail-spots; Gill: this was all visionary, Jacob still in a dream, but so impressed on his mind that he was spoken to and bid to observe and take notice; John recording the Angel bidding Jacob lift up his eyes and see the marked rams, and saying he had seen all that Laban did to him — the Angel revealing both the cause of the increase and his watchful care over Jacob's wrongs (the divine Angel both showing Jacob the secret of his prosperity and declaring his own watchful concern: 'Lift up now thine eyes, and see, all the rams which leap upon the cattle are ringstraked, speckled, and grisled: for I have seen all that Laban doeth unto thee'; the two parts of the Angel's word, first that he reveals the marked offspring, Jacob's wages, came from God's own ordering of the rams not from Jacob's rods, 'for I have seen' the whole matter, and second the tender assurance, 'I have seen all that Laban doeth unto thee,' that God had taken notice of every wrong Laban had done his servant; the comfort of an all-seeing God who marks the injustice done to his own and rises to right it, so Jacob is taught that his increase was God's gift and his wrongs God's concern).

themes: Lift up now thine eyes, and see, all the rams... are ringstraked, speckled, and grisled
for I have seen all that Laban doeth unto thee
the Angel revealing the increase and his watchful care
the comfort of an all-seeing God who marks injustice

schools.summary: Gill: the same Angel that appeared to Jacob in a dream at the beginning of his six years' servitude now appears at its close, declaring himself the God of Bethel, that is, the God who manifested himself to him at Bethel (as Onkelos and Jonathan paraphrase); John recording the Angel saying, 'I am the God of Bethel... now arise, get thee out from this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred' — the Angel revealing himself as Jacob's covenant God of Bethel and renewing the call home (the divine Angel now declaring his full identity and renewing the command: 'I am the God of Bethel, where thou anointedst the pillar, and where thou vowedst a vow unto me'; the force of this self-revelation, that the Angel is none other than the God who met Jacob at Bethel twenty years before, when Jacob, fleeing from Esau, saw the ladder, anointed the pillar, and vowed his vow, this same God now reminding Jacob of that place, that pillar, and that vow, calling him to remember his own covenant engagements, and renewing the command, 'now arise, get thee out from this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred'; God, who keeps his own promises made at Bethel, now calling Jacob to keep his, returning to the land of promise, so the God of Bethel both directs and remembers, and bids Jacob homeward).

themes: I am the God of Bethel, where thou anointedst the pillar, and where thou vowedst a vow unto me
now arise, get thee out from this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred
the God of Bethel renewing the call home
reminding Jacob of his vow made twenty years before

schools.summary: Gill: Rachel and Leah answered one after another, their answers agreeing, and they are put together; it may be Rachel answered in the name of Leah and for herself, since she is mentioned first and the verb is singular (the Targum of Jonathan: Rachel answered with the consent of Leah); John recording Rachel and Leah answering together, 'Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?' — the wives' ready consent, owning they had no more to expect from Laban (the sisters, so often rivals, now answering Jacob with one voice and ready agreement: 'Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?'; the force of their question, that it is a way of saying there is nothing left for them in their father's house, no portion, no inheritance to hold them back, Laban having so dealt with them that they could expect nothing from him and therefore had no reason to cling to his house or oppose Jacob's departure; the unity of the sisters in this, both agreeing to go with Jacob, freed from any tie to their father by his own ill-dealing, so Rachel and Leah give their full and willing consent to the homeward move, owning Laban had left them nothing to stay for).

themes: and Rachel and Leah answered and said unto him
Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?
the wives' united consent to depart
owning Laban had left them nothing to stay for

schools.summary: Gill: Laban had not treated them as children, nor even as freeborn persons, but as if they were foreigners he had taken in war or bought of others, or at least born bondmaids in his house, so that he had a right to sell them; Keil and Delitzsch note the daughters' grievance that Laban had sold them and consumed their dowry; John recording Rachel and Leah complaining that their father counted them as strangers, had sold them, and had devoured their money — the daughters' grievance that Laban had used them as chattels and swallowed their dowry (the sisters justifying their readiness to leave by recounting Laban's hard usage of them: 'Are we not counted of him strangers? for he hath sold us, and hath quite devoured also our money'; the bitterness of the complaint, that Laban had treated his own daughters not as children to be loved and endowed but as 'strangers,' even as if they were bondmaids he could 'sell,' for in giving them to Jacob in exchange for fourteen years' labour he had as good as sold them, and then 'quite devoured' the money or dowry that should have been theirs, keeping it all to himself; the daughters, thus wronged and disinherited by their own father, feeling no tie of duty or affection binding them to his house, so Laban's covetous, unnatural dealing had alienated his own children and justified their departure with Jacob).

themes: Are we not counted of him strangers?
for he hath sold us, and hath quite devoured also our money
the daughters' grievance of Laban's hard usage
his covetous dealing alienating his own children

schools.summary: Calvin: Rachel and Leah confirm the speech of Jacob, but yet in a profane and common manner, not with a lively and pure sense of religion, for they only make a passing allusion to the fact that God had taken the riches; Gill: all the riches which God had taken from their father and given to Jacob for his labour were theirs and their children's; John recording the sisters saying the riches God took from their father were theirs and their children's, and bidding Jacob do whatever God had said — the wives' full consent, owning the wealth as rightfully theirs and submitting to God's command (Rachel and Leah closing their answer with full agreement: 'all the riches which God hath taken from our father, that is ours and our children's: now then, whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do'; two things in their reply, first their claim that the wealth God had transferred from Laban was justly theirs and their children's, the inheritance their father had withheld now given by God, so they had nothing to lose by leaving, and second their submission, 'whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do,' bidding Jacob obey the divine command without delay; Calvin's caution, that the sisters speak rightly yet in a 'profane and common manner,' with only a passing nod to religion rather than a deep and pure faith, so, mixed motives notwithstanding, Rachel and Leah give their consent and urge Jacob's obedience to God).

debates.question: With what spirit do Rachel and Leah confirm Jacob's words?

sides.position: Rightly, yet in a profane and common manner — with only a passing allusion to God, not a lively and pure sense of religion

themes: all the riches which God hath taken from our father, that is ours and our children's
now then, whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do
the wives' consent and call to obey God
owning the wealth as theirs, yet (Calvin) with imperfect faith

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here is Jacob's flight from Laban; we may suppose he had been long considering of it and casting about in his mind respecting it, but when now at last the call came he put it into action; Gill: Jacob rose up and went with them to Laban's house, where his children were, as is plain from Rachel's theft; John recording Jacob rising up and setting his sons and wives upon camels — the resolute beginning of the journey, the family mounted for the long road home (his wives consenting and God commanding, Jacob acting without delay: 'Jacob rose up, and set his sons and his wives upon camels'; the decisiveness of the move, that Jacob, having long pondered his departure, now 'rose up' and put it into action, mounting 'his sons and his wives upon camels' for the long journey from Padan-aram to Canaan; the order of his care, that he provides for the safe and orderly travel of his household, the women and children set upon camels for the hard road, so the verse marks the actual setting-out of Jacob and his family on the homeward way that God had appointed).

themes: Then Jacob rose up, and set his sons and his wives upon camels
the resolute beginning of the homeward journey
mounting his family for the long road to Canaan
Jacob acting without delay on God's command

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he carried away all his cattle and all his goods which he had gotten, the cattle of his getting which he had gotten in Padan-aram, to go to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan; Gill: he carried away his sheep, camels, and asses, the Jews saying he had 5,500 head of cattle; John recording Jacob carrying away all his cattle and goods gotten in Padan-aram, to go to Isaac his father in Canaan — the gathering up of all his lawful gains for the journey to his father's land (Jacob taking with him the whole of his hard-earned substance: 'all his cattle, and all his goods which he had gotten, the cattle of his getting, which he had gotten in Padanaram'; the emphasis on the lawfulness of his wealth, that it is repeatedly called what 'he had gotten' by his own labour and God's blessing in Padan-aram, not anything stolen or taken wrongfully from Laban; the goal of the journey, 'to go to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan,' the land of promise, returning at last to his aged father after twenty years' absence, so Jacob departs with all that was honestly his, set upon reaching his father and the land God had given his fathers).

themes: and he carried away all his cattle, and all his goods which he had gotten
the cattle of his getting, which he had gotten in Padanaram
for to go to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan
Jacob departing with all his lawful, hard-won substance

schools.summary: Gill: Laban went to shear his sheep, which were under the care of his sons and were three days' distance from Jacob's flocks, giving Jacob a fair opportunity to depart since Laban and his sons were at such a distance; Rachel stole the images (teraphim) that were her father's; John recording that Laban went to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole the images that were her father's — the providential occasion of Laban's absence and Rachel's puzzling theft of the teraphim (two things falling together: 'Laban went to shear his sheep: and Rachel had stolen the images that were her father's'; the providence in Laban's absence, that the shearing took him and his sons three days' journey away, giving Jacob the opportunity to depart unhindered; the puzzling act of Rachel, who 'stolen the images,' the teraphim, her father's household idols, the commentators differing on her motive, some that she took them out of lingering superstition or a regard for them as objects of worship, others that she meant to keep her father from consulting them to divine the route Jacob had taken, others that she wished to wean Laban from his idolatry by removing them, others that she valued them for the gold or silver of which they were made, but whatever her reason the act being a fault that would bring trouble).

debates.question: Why did Rachel steal her father's teraphim (household images)?

sides.position: Out of lingering superstition or regard for them as objects of worship
To keep Laban from consulting them to divine the route Jacob had taken
To wean her father from his idolatry by removing them
For the value of the gold or silver of which they were made

themes: and Laban went to shear his sheep
and Rachel had stolen the images that were her father's
Laban's providential absence and Rachel's theft of the teraphim
the act a fault, of uncertain motive, that would bring trouble

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob went away without Laban's knowledge, or giving him any notice of it; he was too cunning for Laban the Syrian, who, notwithstanding his astrology and superstitious arts which the Syrians are addicted to, had no foresight of this matter; John recording that Jacob stole away unawares to Laban the Syrian, not telling him that he fled — the secret departure that outwitted the crafty Laban (Jacob, to avoid being hindered, leaving without warning: 'Jacob stole away unawares to Laban the Syrian, in that he told him not that he fled'; the manner of the going, that Jacob 'stole away unawares,' secretly, without giving Laban any notice of his flight; the touch the commentators add, that Jacob by this secret departure 'was too cunning for Laban the Syrian,' who for all his boasted astrology and divining arts had no foreknowledge of the matter and was taken wholly by surprise; that, while the secrecy may be questioned, it is set against the background of Laban's fraud and the real danger that he would have hindered or robbed Jacob had he known, so Jacob slips away from the deceiver who had so long deceived him).

themes: and Jacob stole away unawares to Laban the Syrian
in that he told him not that he fled
Jacob's secret departure outwitting Laban
the crafty Syrian, for all his arts, taken by surprise

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he fled with all that he had, rose up, passed over the river (the Euphrates), and set his face toward the mount Gilead; Gill: he fled with all that he had, his wives, his children, cattle, and substance; John recording Jacob fleeing with all that he had, passing over the river and setting his face toward mount Gilead — the swift flight homeward, crossing the Euphrates and pressing on toward Gilead (Jacob making all speed on his way: 'he fled with all that he had; and he rose up, and passed over the river, and set his face toward the mount Gilead'; the haste and resolve of the journey, that Jacob 'fled' with his whole company and substance, 'passed over the river,' the Euphrates the great boundary, and 'set his face toward the mount Gilead,' fixing his course steadily homeward; the determination in the phrase 'set his face,' that Jacob, having begun, pressed forward without looking back, making for Gilead on the way to Canaan, so the flight is well begun, the great river behind him and the hill-country of Gilead before him).

themes: so he fled with all that he had... and passed over the river
and set his face toward the mount Gilead
Jacob's swift flight across the Euphrates
the determined journey homeward, pressing forward

schools.summary: Gill: three days after Jacob was gone Laban had the report of it, by some means or another, by some of his neighbours or servants left at home, and sooner he could not well have it, since the flock he went to shear was three days' distance; John recording that it was told Laban on the third day that Jacob had fled — the delayed report giving Jacob a head start on his journey (Laban not learning of the flight at once: 'it was told Laban on the third day that Jacob was fled'; the providence in the delay, that because Laban was away at the sheep-shearing, three days' journey distant, the news of Jacob's departure could not reach him until 'the third day'; this giving Jacob a valuable head start, three days' journey toward home before Laban even knew he was gone; the hand of God in the timing, ordering events so that Jacob might get well on his way before his pursuer could set out, so the delay of the report is itself a providence furthering Jacob's escape).

themes: and it was told Laban on the third day that Jacob was fled
the delayed report giving Jacob a head start
a providence in the timing furthering his escape
three days' journey homeward before Laban knew

schools.summary: Gill: he took his brethren with him, some of his relations, the descendants of his father's brethren, the sons of Nahor, of whom there were seven, or some of his neighbours and acquaintance whom he called to assist him; John recording Laban taking his brethren and pursuing Jacob seven days' journey, overtaking him in mount Gilead — the angry pursuit of Laban, hunting down the fleeing Jacob (Laban, on learning of the flight, setting out in hot pursuit: 'he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him seven days' journey; and they overtook him in the mount Gilead'; the energy and menace of the pursuit, that Laban gathers 'his brethren,' his kinsmen and neighbours, into a band, and 'pursued after him seven days' journey,' a long and determined chase, pressing on until 'they overtook him in the mount Gilead'; the gathering of an armed company suggesting no friendly purpose but anger and intent to seize Jacob or his goods, and that, but for God's intervening, this overtaking might have ended in violence, so the deceiver, now the pursuer, overtakes Jacob in Gilead with a hostile band).

themes: and he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him seven days' journey
and they overtook him in the mount Gilead
Laban's hot pursuit with a band of kinsmen
the armed company suggesting anger and hostile intent

schools.summary: Gill: it is probable that Laban came to mount Gilead late in the evening, and so had no sight of or conversation with Jacob until the morning, and that night God came to him in a dream and advised him; John recording God coming to Laban the Syrian in a dream by night, warning him to speak to Jacob neither good nor bad — the divine intervention restraining Laban's hand before he could harm Jacob (just as Laban overtakes Jacob with hostile intent, God himself stepping in: 'God came to Laban the Syrian in a dream by night, and said unto him, Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad'; the timeliness and power of this intervention, that God comes to the very man who meant Jacob harm, and that 'by night' before any meeting could take place, charging him, 'Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad'; the force of the phrase 'good or bad,' a Hebrew way of saying Laban was to attempt nothing at all against Jacob, neither by force nor by fair words to do him hurt or turn him from his way; the fulfilment of God's promise 'I will be with thee,' God bridling the pursuer and protecting his servant, so the God who called Jacob homeward now guards him, restraining Laban's hand).

themes: and God came to Laban the Syrian in a dream by night
Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad
God restraining the pursuer's hand to protect Jacob
the fulfilment of 'I will be with thee'

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here is the reasoning, not to say the rallying, that took place between Laban and Jacob at their meeting in the mount; Gill: Laban, having come to the mount the night before, now in the morning came nearer to Jacob so as to hold a conversation with him; John recording Laban overtaking Jacob, who had pitched his tent in the mount, and Laban with his brethren pitching in mount Gilead — the two parties encamped face to face in Gilead, set for the confrontation (the pursuit closing with the two companies pitched over against each other: 'Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mount: and Laban with his brethren pitched in the mount of Gilead'; the scene now set, Jacob's company encamped on the mount and Laban with his armed band pitching nearby, the two parties face to face for the meeting that must follow; that, though Laban had overtaken Jacob, God's warning the night before had already drawn the sting from the encounter, so that what might have been an attack becomes a confrontation in words, so the stage is set in Gilead for the reckoning between Jacob and Laban, but under God's restraining hand).

themes: Then Laban overtook Jacob... Jacob had pitched his tent in the mount
and Laban with his brethren pitched in the mount of Gilead
the two parties encamped face to face in Gilead
the confrontation disarmed beforehand by God's warning

schools.summary: Gill: Laban said this to Jacob upon their meeting together, perhaps in some middle place between their two tents; John recording Laban demanding of Jacob what he had done, stealing away and carrying off his daughters as captives of war — Laban's indignant reproach, painting Jacob as a thief and abductor (Laban, though restrained from violence, opening with bitter words of complaint: 'What hast thou done, that thou hast stolen away unawares to me, and carried away my daughters, as captives taken with the sword?'; the two charges in his reproach, first that Jacob had 'stolen away unawares,' departing secretly like a thief without leave or notice, and second that he had 'carried away' Laban's 'daughters, as captives taken with the sword,' as though Jacob had seized them by force like prisoners of war rather than taking his own lawful wives; the exaggeration and injured tone of the complaint, Laban casting himself as the wronged father robbed of his children; the irony, that Laban, who had himself treated those daughters as chattels, now poses as their loving and aggrieved father, so Laban opens the confrontation with reproachful and self-justifying words).

themes: What hast thou done, that thou hast stolen away unawares to me
and carried away my daughters, as captives taken with the sword?
Laban's indignant reproach of Jacob
the injured, exaggerated complaint of one who used them as chattels

schools.summary: Gill: Laban intimates that he would not have been against Jacob's departure had he but acquainted him with it and the reasons of it, so that he had no need to use such privacy and go away like a thief by stealth; John recording Laban asking why Jacob fled secretly, when he would have sent him away with mirth, songs, tabret, and harp — Laban's hollow profession of a festive farewell he would never truly have given (Laban continuing his complaint with a show of injured kindness: 'Wherefore didst thou flee away secretly, and steal away from me; and didst not tell me, that I might have sent thee away with mirth, and with songs, with tabret, and with harp?'; the professed grievance, that Laban pretends he would gladly have given Jacob a joyful, honourable send-off 'with mirth, and with songs, with tabret, and with harp,' a festive farewell with music and rejoicing, had only Jacob told him; the hollowness of the claim, that given Laban's proven fraud, his changed countenance, and the hostile band he brought, this profession of a glad farewell rings false, Jacob's very reason for secrecy being that he knew Laban would have hindered, not feasted, his going, so Laban's words of injured kindness mask the truth of his grudging and hostile heart).

themes: Wherefore didst thou flee away secretly, and steal away from me
that I might have sent thee away with mirth, and with songs, with tabret, and with harp?
Laban's hollow profession of a festive farewell
words of injured kindness masking his grudging heart

schools.summary: Gill: Laban complains he had no opportunity to take his farewell, which used to be done with a kiss, as it is with us at this day; by his sons he means his grandsons (so the Targum of Jonathan, 'my daughters' sons'), and by his daughters, Rachel and Leah; John recording Laban complaining that he was not allowed to kiss his sons and daughters, and charging Jacob with having done foolishly — Laban's final reproach over the denied farewell, calling Jacob's flight folly (Laban pressing the grievance of the parting kiss denied him: 'hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and my daughters? thou hast now done foolishly in so doing'; the complaint, that Laban laments that Jacob's secret flight robbed him of the customary farewell, the affectionate parting kiss to his 'sons,' his grandsons, and 'daughters,' Rachel and Leah, as was the manner at leave-taking, and caps his reproach with a charge of folly, 'thou hast now done foolishly in so doing'; Laban speaking as a wronged and tender grandfather, yet the reader, knowing his fraud and his armed pursuit, hearing the self-serving tone beneath, Laban's whole speech mingling real and pretended grievance, casting Jacob as the wrongdoer while passing over his own long deceit, so Laban closes his opening reproach with the charge that Jacob had acted foolishly).

themes: and hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and my daughters?
thou hast now done foolishly in so doing
Laban's final reproach over the denied farewell
the self-serving tone beneath the tender grandfather's complaint

schools.summary: Gill: Laban means it was in the power of his hand to do Jacob and his family — wives, children, and servants — hurt, who were not able to stand against Laban and the men he brought with him (the Jerusalem Targum paraphrasing, 'I have an army and a multitude,' a large force); but the God of Jacob's father had warned him; John recording Laban telling Jacob it was in his power to hurt him, but the God of his father had warned him yesternight to speak neither good nor bad — the boast of power checked by the confession of God's overruling restraint (Laban, in the same breath, threatening and yet owning himself bridled: 'It is in the power of my hand to do you hurt: but the God of your father spake unto me yesternight, saying, Take thou heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad'; the two parts, first the proud boast that Laban had the force, 'the power of my hand,' to crush the defenceless Jacob and his family, and second the humbling confession that 'the God of your father' had appeared to him 'yesternight,' the night before, and charged him to attempt nothing against Jacob; the testimony to providence even from the lips of an enemy, Laban himself being made to acknowledge that the God of Jacob's fathers had stayed his hand, so the boast of the powerful is overruled by the word of the Almighty who guards his own).

themes: It is in the power of my hand to do you hurt
but the God of your father spake unto me yesternight
the boast of power checked by God's restraint
the enemy himself owning that God stayed his hand

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Laban grants that Jacob sore longed after his father's house, yet asks wherefore he had stolen his gods; Gill: Jacob was determined upon going and in haste to do it; John recording Laban granting Jacob's longing for home yet demanding why he had stolen his gods — the charge of theft of the teraphim, exposing Laban's idolatry (Laban turning from the matter of the flight to a new accusation: 'though thou wouldest needs be gone, because thou sore longedst after thy father's house, yet wherefore hast thou stolen my gods?'; the two things in his words, first a grudging allowance that he could understand Jacob's homesickness, his 'sore' longing 'after thy father's house,' as some excuse for departing, but second a sharp charge of sacrilege, 'wherefore hast thou stolen my gods?' the teraphim, the household images Rachel had taken; the unwitting confession in Laban's own mouth, that he calls the teraphim 'my gods,' betraying his idolatry, that he who should have known the true God worshipped images that could be stolen; the irony that a man's 'gods' could be carried off by a thief, exposing the folly of idols, so Laban's charge reveals his own idolatrous heart).

themes: thou sore longedst after thy father's house
yet wherefore hast thou stolen my gods?
Laban's charge of stealing his gods
his own word 'my gods' betraying his idolatry

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob feared that Laban would have done all he could to hinder him from going away himself, and not only so but would have prevented his taking his daughters with him, and especially would have detained his cattle; John recording Jacob answering that he fled because he was afraid Laban would take his daughters from him by force — the honest reason for the secret flight, fear of Laban's violence (Jacob replying first to the charge of secret flight: 'Because I was afraid: for I said, Peradventure thou wouldest take by force thy daughters from me'; the candour of the answer, that Jacob owns plainly the true reason for his stealing away, it being fear, and a well-grounded fear, that Laban, had he known, would have used force to keep Jacob from going and to take back his daughters and his cattle; Jacob's fear being no groundless suspicion but a just reading of Laban's covetous and overbearing character, proved by the armed band Laban brought in pursuit, so Jacob defends his secret departure as the prudent act of a man fearing violence from a powerful and unscrupulous father-in-law).

themes: Because I was afraid
Peradventure thou wouldest take by force thy daughters from me
Jacob's honest reason for the secret flight
a well-grounded fear of Laban's violence

schools.summary: Gill: this is the answer to Laban's last question; Jacob knew nothing of their being taken away by any, and thought himself safe in saying what he did, being confident that no one of his household had them; John recording Jacob declaring that whoever was found with the gods should not live, and inviting Laban to search, for Jacob knew not Rachel had stolen them — the rash oath spoken in ignorance, which unknowingly imperilled Rachel (Jacob, sure of his innocence, answering Laban's charge of theft with a bold challenge: 'With whomsoever thou findest thy gods, let him not live: before our brethren discern thou what is thine with me, and take it to thee'; the confidence and danger of his words, that Jacob, certain that none of his household would take such things, pronounces a heavy oath, 'let him not live,' and freely invites Laban to search and take whatever was his, but the narrator adding the ominous note, 'For Jacob knew not that Rachel had stolen them'; the peril Jacob unwittingly called down, his rash word dooming the very wife he loved, some of the commentators seeing in this oath a cause that hastened Rachel's later death, a warning against hasty oaths spoken in ignorance, so Jacob's confident challenge, just in intent, unknowingly imperilled Rachel).

debates.question: Did Jacob's rash oath ('let him not live') bear on Rachel's later death?

sides.position: Some commentators see in this oath, spoken in ignorance, a cause that hastened Rachel's later death in childbirth
Jacob spoke in good conscience, certain none of his household was guilty; the note 'Jacob knew not' clears his intent

themes: With whomsoever thou findest thy gods, let him not live
For Jacob knew not that Rachel had stolen them
Jacob's rash oath spoken in confident ignorance
unknowingly imperilling the wife he loved

schools.summary: Gill: Laban went into Jacob's tent first, where he most suspected they were, being taken not out of any value for them but contempt of them; he searched Jacob's, Leah's, and the two maidservants' tents, found nothing, and then entered Rachel's; John recording Laban searching Jacob's tent, Leah's, and the two maidservants', finding nothing, then entering Rachel's tent — the diligent but fruitless search, drawing ever nearer to the hidden images (Laban, taking up Jacob's challenge, searching the tents one by one: 'Laban went into Jacob's tent, and into Leah's tent, and into the two maidservants' tents; but he found them not. Then went he out of Leah's tent, and entered into Rachel's tent'; the thoroughness of the search, Laban going from tent to tent, Jacob's first as most suspected, then Leah's, then the maidservants', finding nothing, and at last coming to 'Rachel's tent' where the images in fact lay hidden; the rising tension of the narrative, the searcher drawing ever closer to the very place where the teraphim were concealed, all unknown to Jacob, that, had the images been found, Jacob's oath would have fallen on Rachel, so the search closes in on Rachel's tent where the stolen gods were hidden).

themes: Laban went into Jacob's tent, and into Leah's tent
but he found them not
Laban's search closing in on Rachel's tent
the rising tension as the searcher nears the hidden images

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Rachel had put the teraphim in the camel's saddle and sat upon them, so that Laban searched the whole tent but found them not; Gill: Rachel, hearing her father inquire about them and her husband give leave to search and to put to death whoever should be found to have them, took them from the place where she had laid them and hid them; John recording Rachel having hidden the images in the camel's furniture and sitting upon them, so that Laban searched all the tent in vain — the cunning concealment by which Rachel hid the teraphim under herself (Rachel, to escape discovery, resorting to a clever and irreverent hiding-place: 'Rachel had taken the images, and put them in the camel's furniture, and sat upon them. And Laban searched all the tent, but found them not'; the device, that Rachel placed the teraphim in 'the camel's furniture,' the saddle or pannier, and 'sat upon them,' so that her father, searching everywhere else, never thought to look beneath her; the unintended mockery of the idols, that the very 'gods' Laban prized so highly were sat upon and hidden beneath a woman, a silent witness to their worthlessness, that things so treated could be no true gods, so Rachel's stratagem succeeded and the search failed, the teraphim, dishonoured beneath Rachel, escaping Laban's searching hand).

themes: Rachel had taken the images, and put them in the camel's furniture, and sat upon them
And Laban searched all the tent, but found them not
Rachel's cunning concealment of the teraphim
the idols sat upon, a silent witness to their worthlessness

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'the custom of women is upon me' — this she knew must be a satisfactory reason to her father, for if the teraphim were used for any religious purpose, and they seem to have been so used, contact with a woman in such a state would defile them, so Laban would not press her to rise; Gill notes she said this as he approached nearer, having searched her tent all over; John recording Rachel asking her father's pardon for not rising, pleading the custom of women, so that he searched but found not the images — the artful plea that completed the concealment (Rachel covering her sitting still with a plausible and disarming excuse: 'Let it not displease my lord that I cannot rise up before thee; for the custom of women is upon me'; the cleverness of the plea, that Rachel, sitting on the hidden teraphim, begs pardon for not rising in respect to her father, giving as her reason 'the custom of women,' her monthly courses, a reason at once natural, modest, and, as Clarke notes, the more fitting because if the teraphim were objects of worship such uncleanness near them would be thought to defile them, so Laban would not press her to rise; the success of the stratagem, 'he searched, but found not the images,' the irony again that the very excuse touching ceremonial uncleanness shielded the idols, doubly dishonouring them, so Rachel's plea completes the hiding and the search ends in failure).

themes: Let it not displease my lord that I cannot rise up before thee
for the custom of women is upon me
Rachel's artful plea completing the concealment
the excuse doubly dishonouring the idols she sat upon

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob, having answered Laban's questions to the silencing of him, and nothing of his being found upon search, took heart and was of good courage and in high spirits, and in his turn was heated also, and perhaps more than he should, demanding what his trespass and sin was; John recording Jacob, now angry, chiding Laban and demanding what his trespass or sin was, that Laban had so hotly pursued him — the turning of the tables, Jacob's just anger when proved innocent (Jacob, his innocence now established by the fruitless search, turning from defence to bold reproach: 'Jacob was wroth, and chode with Laban... What is my trespass? what is my sin, that thou hast so hotly pursued after me?'; the change in Jacob, that where before he had answered Laban's charges in fear, now, the false accusation of theft disproved, he takes courage and in righteous indignation demands of Laban what wrong he had ever done to deserve so hot and hostile a pursuit; the force of the questions, 'What is my trespass? what is my sin?' a challenge to Laban to name any real fault, knowing he could name none, so Jacob, cleared of the charge, justly turns the reproach back upon his accuser).

themes: And Jacob was wroth, and chode with Laban
What is my trespass? what is my sin, that thou hast so hotly pursued after me?
Jacob's righteous indignation when proved innocent
the tables turned, the accused now the accuser

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob challenges Laban, that having searched all his stuff, what had he found? let him set it before their brethren to judge between them; Gill: Jacob speaks of all his vessels or utensils, whether household goods or such as were used with regard to the cattle, or armour for defence; John recording Jacob demanding what Laban had found of all his goods, and bidding him set it before the kinsmen to judge between them — the open appeal to impartial witnesses, confident of his innocence (Jacob, sure that nothing of Laban's was found, pressing his advantage with a bold and open challenge: 'Whereas thou hast searched all my stuff, what hast thou found of all thy household stuff? set it here before my brethren and thy brethren, that they may judge betwixt us both'; the confidence of a clear conscience, that Jacob invites Laban to produce, in the sight of all, anything of his that the search had turned up, and to lay it 'before my brethren and thy brethren,' the kinsmen of both present as witnesses, that they might 'judge' the matter justly; that a guilty man would never make such an open appeal to impartial judges, but Jacob, conscious of his innocence, courts the fullest examination, so Jacob's challenge to public judgment proves the clearness of his cause).

themes: what hast thou found of all thy household stuff?
set it here before my brethren and thy brethren, that they may judge betwixt us both
Jacob's open appeal to impartial judgment
the confidence of a clear conscience courting examination

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob had now been with Laban twenty years, so that he must have been ninety-seven years of age; John recording Jacob recounting that for twenty years he had served Laban, that the ewes and she-goats had not miscarried, and that he had not eaten the rams of the flock — the proof of Jacob's diligent and honest shepherding through twenty years (Jacob, his innocence cleared, laying before Laban the long record of his faithful service: 'This twenty years have I been with thee; thy ewes and thy she goats have not cast their young, and the rams of thy flock have I not eaten'; the marks of an exemplary shepherd, that through 'twenty years' Jacob had so tended Laban's flock that 'thy ewes and thy she goats have not cast their young,' none miscarrying under his careful keeping, a sign of skilful and diligent care, and he had been so honest that 'the rams of thy flock have I not eaten,' never taking even of the flock for his own food as a careless or covetous keeper might; Jacob's defence being a portrait of perfect fidelity, sparing himself nothing and taking nothing wrongfully, so Jacob's twenty years of service stand as a witness to his integrity against Laban's charges).

themes: This twenty years have I been with thee
thy ewes and thy she goats have not cast their young
the rams of thy flock have I not eaten
Jacob's twenty years of faithful, careful shepherding

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'of my hand didst thou require it' more particularly marks the covetous and unfeeling disposition of Laban, who made Jacob bear losses he was not bound to bear; Gill: Jacob did not bring the torn beast to show what had befallen it, that it might appear he had one the less to account for, but bore the loss himself; John recording Jacob saying that what was torn of beasts he did not bring but bore the loss himself, Laban requiring it of his hand whether stolen by day or night — the hard exaction by which Laban made Jacob pay for losses beyond his control (Jacob recounting a further proof of his fidelity and of Laban's harshness: 'That which was torn of beasts I brought not unto thee; I bare the loss of it; of my hand didst thou require it, whether stolen by day, or stolen by night'; the custom and the exaction, that by ordinary right and the later law a shepherd was not held to answer for what was 'torn of beasts' if he brought the torn carcase as proof, but Laban, covetous and unfeeling as Clarke notes, exacted even this of Jacob, making him 'bare the loss' himself and 'require it of my hand,' whether the beast was lost 'by day' or 'by night'; Jacob shouldering every loss, even those no justice could lay on him, rather than wrong Laban, so Jacob's patience under Laban's hard dealing is a further witness to his integrity and to Laban's greed).

themes: that which was torn of beasts I brought not unto thee; I bare the loss of it
of my hand didst thou require it, whether stolen by day, or stolen by night
Jacob bearing losses beyond his fault
Laban covetously exacting even what justice could not lay on him

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: being exposed to the heat by day and frost by night is made part of the heaviest punishment, and Jacob endured both for Laban's sake; Gill: Jacob was in such a situation, an uncomfortable plight and condition; John recording Jacob describing how the drought consumed him by day and the frost by night, and his sleep departed from his eyes — the picture of a shepherd's bitter toil, scorched by day and frozen by night (Jacob painting the hardship of his long service in vivid terms: 'in the day the drought consumed me, and the frost by night; and my sleep departed from mine eyes'; the severity of the toil, that Jacob, keeping the flocks in the open, endured the extremes of climate, 'in the day the drought,' the scorching, parching heat, 'consumed me,' and 'the frost by night,' the bitter, biting cold of the desert nights, chilled him, while 'my sleep departed from mine eyes,' broken rest watching over the flock through the night; Jacob sparing himself nothing, bearing heat, cold, and sleeplessness for Laban's profit, and Clarke's word that such exposure was reckoned among the heaviest of sufferings, yet Jacob bore it faithfully, so Jacob's recital of his hardships magnifies both his fidelity and Laban's ingratitude).

themes: in the day the drought consumed me, and the frost by night
and my sleep departed from mine eyes
the shepherd's bitter toil of heat, cold, and sleeplessness
Jacob sparing himself nothing for Laban's profit

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob served fourteen years for the two daughters and six years for the cattle, and Laban changed his wages ten times; Gill: Jacob had been twenty years in Laban's house, attended with these difficulties, inconveniences, and hardships; John recording Jacob summing up his twenty years, fourteen for the two daughters and six for the cattle, and Laban's changing his wages ten times — the full reckoning of Jacob's service against Laban's repeated fraud (Jacob bringing his complaint to a head with a precise accounting: 'Thus have I been twenty years in thy house; I served thee fourteen years for thy two daughters, and six years for thy cattle: and thou hast changed my wages ten times'; the exact tally, 'twenty years' in all, divided as 'fourteen years for thy two daughters,' the seven years each for Leah and Rachel, 'and six years for thy cattle,' the later term for a share of the flock, and against this long faithful service standing Laban's repeated treachery, 'thou hast changed my wages ten times,' again and again altering the bargain to cheat Jacob of his due; the sharp contrast Jacob draws between his own steady fidelity and Laban's restless fraud, so the reckoning lays bare the injustice Jacob suffered through twenty years).

themes: I served thee fourteen years for thy two daughters, and six years for thy cattle
and thou hast changed my wages ten times
Jacob's full reckoning of his twenty years
the contrast of Jacob's fidelity and Laban's repeated fraud

schools.summary: Gill: one and the same God is meant, who was the God of his father Isaac, and before him the God of Abraham, and now 'the fear of Isaac,' whom Isaac feared and served with reverence and godly fear; John recording Jacob declaring that, but for the God of Abraham and the fear of Isaac, Laban would have sent him away empty, but God saw his affliction and rebuked Laban — the crowning confession that God alone had preserved and vindicated Jacob (Jacob closing his great defence by giving all the glory to God: 'Except the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac, had been with me, surely thou hadst sent me away now empty'; the rich naming of God, 'the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac,' the covenant God of his fathers, here called 'the fear of Isaac,' the God whom Isaac reverenced; Jacob's confession that, left to Laban, he would have been stripped and 'sent away now empty,' but God had kept and prospered him, and the vindication, 'God hath seen mine affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked thee yesternight,' the all-seeing God having marked Jacob's affliction and toil and openly rebuked Laban in the dream, so Jacob crowns the whole with the confession that God alone had preserved, prospered, and vindicated him against Laban).

themes: Except the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac, had been with me
surely thou hadst sent me away now empty
God hath seen mine affliction... and rebuked thee yesternight
Jacob's confession that God alone preserved and vindicated him

schools.summary: Gill: Laban neither denied the truth of what Jacob had said, nor acknowledged any fault he had been guilty of, nor asked forgiveness for it, though he seemed to be convicted in his own conscience of it; John recording Laban claiming the daughters, children, and cattle as all his, yet asking what he could do to them — the empty boast of ownership that ends in a confession of helplessness (Laban, unable to answer Jacob's defence, falling back on a hollow claim of ownership: 'These daughters are my daughters, and these children are my children, and these cattle are my cattle, and all that thou seest is mine'; the emptiness of the boast, that Laban asserts a sweeping claim to all, the daughters, the grandchildren, the flocks, even 'all that thou seest,' yet in the same breath confesses he can do nothing, 'what can I do this day unto these my daughters, or unto these children which they have born?'; that Laban, restrained by God and unable to refute Jacob, neither confesses his fault nor lets go his pride but covers his helplessness with a claim that changes nothing; the working of God, who had so bridled Laban that his very boast ends in surrender, so Laban's grand claim collapses into a confession that he could do nothing against Jacob).

themes: These daughters are my daughters, and these children are my children
what can I do this day unto these my daughters, or unto these children which they have born?
Laban's sweeping claim collapsing into helplessness
God having bridled him so his boast ends in surrender

schools.summary: Gill: Laban, perceiving that Jacob's God was with him and blessed him and made him prosperous and protected him, proposed they be good friends and enter into an alliance for mutual safety, and make an agreement for each other's good; John recording Laban proposing that he and Jacob make a covenant, to be a witness between them — the turn from accusation to a covenant of peace, born of Laban's seeing God was with Jacob (Laban, finding he can neither harm nor refute Jacob, changing his tone from threat to treaty: 'Now therefore come thou, let us make a covenant, I and thou; and let it be for a witness between me and thee'; the reason behind the proposal, that Laban, perceiving God was with Jacob, blessing and protecting him, judges it wiser to make peace than to continue the quarrel, and so proposes 'a covenant,' a formal agreement of mutual safety and non-aggression, 'for a witness between me and thee'; the covenant springing not from love but from Laban's recognition of God's hand on Jacob and his own helplessness, the providence that turns the hostile pursuer into a covenant-maker, so Laban, bridled by God, seeks peace with the man he came to harm).

themes: Now therefore come thou, let us make a covenant, I and thou
and let it be for a witness between me and thee
Laban turning from accusation to a covenant of peace
the pursuer, bridled by God, becoming a covenant-maker

schools.summary: Gill: to show his readiness to agree to the motion, Jacob immediately took a large stone that lay upon the mount and set it up on one end, to be a standing monument or memorial of the agreement now about to be made between them; John recording Jacob taking a stone and setting it up as a pillar — the ready raising of a memorial-stone, Jacob's prompt consent to the covenant (Jacob answering Laban's proposal at once with a deed: 'Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar'; the readiness of Jacob's consent, that without hesitation or grudge Jacob agrees to the covenant of peace and seals his agreement by setting up 'a stone... for a pillar,' a large stone raised on end as a lasting monument and memorial of the agreement now made; the fitness of the act, that a standing stone, visible and enduring, was the customary witness to such covenants, a silent and permanent reminder of the bond; that Jacob, though the wronged party, willingly meets Laban's overture of peace, showing himself ready for reconciliation, so Jacob's setting up of the pillar marks his glad consent to the covenant).

themes: And Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar
the ready raising of a memorial-stone
Jacob's prompt consent to the covenant of peace
the wronged party ready for reconciliation

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob said this to his brethren — not to his sons, as the Targum of Jonathan and Jarchi say, for these would not be called brethren and were too young to gather large stones, but rather his servants, his kinsmen and companions who were with him; John recording Jacob bidding his brethren gather stones, and they made a heap and ate upon it — the raising of the witness-heap and the shared meal that sealed the covenant (besides the single pillar, a heap raised and a meal eaten: 'Jacob said unto his brethren, Gather stones; and they took stones, and made an heap: and they did eat there upon the heap'; the two acts, first the gathering of many stones into 'an heap,' a second and larger monument to witness the covenant, and second 'they did eat there upon the heap,' a covenant-meal, the shared eating that solemnly ratified and sealed the agreement between the two parties as was the ancient custom; the meaning of 'his brethren,' not Jacob's young sons but his kinsmen, servants, and companions who helped raise the heap and shared the meal, that the eating together over the heap bound the covenant in fellowship, so the heap of stones and the common meal together seal the peace between Jacob and Laban).

themes: Gather stones; and they took stones, and made an heap
and they did eat there upon the heap
the witness-heap and the covenant meal
the shared eating ratifying the agreement in fellowship

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'Jegar-sahadutha' is Aramaic (Syriac/Chaldee) for 'the heap of witness,' while 'Galeed' is the Hebrew of the same meaning; Gill: Jegar-sahadutha in the Syriac and Chaldee languages signifies 'an heap of witness,' it being a witness of the covenant between Laban and Jacob; John recording Laban naming the heap Jegar-sahadutha and Jacob naming it Galeed — the two names in two tongues, both meaning 'heap of witness,' an early mark of the parting of the languages (the heap receiving a double name, each in the speaker's own tongue: 'Laban called it Jegarsahadutha: but Jacob called it Galeed'; the meaning and interest of the double naming, that 'Jegar-sahadutha' is Aramaic, the language of Laban the Syrian, and 'Galeed' is Hebrew, the language of Jacob, but both signify exactly the same thing, 'the heap of witness'; this being one of the earliest marks in Scripture of the divergence of the two related tongues, Laban speaking Aramaic and Jacob Hebrew though they were kinsmen, that, whatever the language, the heap stood as a 'witness' between them, its very name declaring its purpose, so the one monument bears two names of one meaning, witnessing the covenant in both their tongues).

themes: And Laban called it Jegarsahadutha: but Jacob called it Galeed
both names meaning 'heap of witness'
Laban's Aramaic and Jacob's Hebrew
an early mark of the parting of the two tongues

schools.summary: Gill: Laban said this heap is a witness of the covenant now about to be made between them that day, and a witness against them should they break it; Adam Clarke thinks the verses are somewhat divided, but the sense is that the heap witnesses the covenant; John recording Laban declaring the heap a witness between them that day, whence its name Galeed — the heap solemnly named as the standing witness of the covenant (Laban putting into words the meaning of the monument: 'This heap is a witness between me and thee this day. Therefore was the name of it called Galeed'; the office of the heap, that it is set up as 'a witness between me and thee,' a standing, silent testimony to the covenant made 'this day,' and, as Gill adds, a witness against either party should he break the agreement; that from this office the heap took its name 'Galeed,' the heap of witness, the name declaring its very purpose; the solemnity of such a witness, an enduring monument that would outlast the men and remind all who saw it of the bond sworn that day, so the heap stands, named and declared, as the abiding witness of the covenant between Jacob and Laban).

themes: This heap is a witness between me and thee this day
Therefore was the name of it called Galeed
the heap declared the standing witness of the covenant
a witness against either who should break it

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Mizpah, 'watchtower,' is named because the parties, distrusting one another, invoke God to keep watch when they are apart; Gill: Mizpah, being a Hebrew word, signifies 'a watch' or 'watchtower,' though by what follows it seems given by Laban, who could speak Hebrew as well as Syriac; John recording the heap also called Mizpah, for Laban said, 'The LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another' — the appeal to God's watchful eye, a watchword of mutual distrust as much as of blessing (the place receiving a further name and a solemn appeal: 'And Mizpah; for he said, The LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another'; the meaning of 'Mizpah,' a watchtower or place of watching, and the words spoken, that 'the LORD' might 'watch between' the two parties when they were 'absent one from another,' each out of the other's sight; the true sense of this often-quoted saying, that it is not, as it is sometimes sentimentally taken, a tender benediction of affection, but a watchword of mutual distrust, since neither could keep an eye on the other once parted, they call on God to watch both, that neither break the covenant or harm the other behind his back, so the appeal to God's all-seeing eye binds the covenant where human watching fails).

debates.question: Is the 'Mizpah benediction' a word of affection or of distrust?

sides.position: A watchword of mutual distrust — the parties, unable to watch one another once parted, invoke God to keep watch lest either break faith
It is often sentimentally taken as a tender benediction of affection, but the context does not support this

themes: And Mizpah; for he said, The LORD watch between me and thee
when we are absent one from another
the appeal to God's watchful eye between distrustful parties
a watchword of distrust, not a tender benediction

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'no man is with us' — though all were present at the sacrifice offered, in the agreement about the daughters it was a matter between Jacob and God alone, no human witness being able to testify how he treats them in private; Gill notes afflicting the daughters in body or mind, by hard blows or ill words or withholding necessaries; John recording Laban charging Jacob not to afflict his daughters nor take other wives, since God is witness between them — the fatherly stipulation guarding the daughters, sealed by the witness of God (Laban's chief concern in the covenant being for his daughters: 'If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives beside my daughters, no man is with us; see, God is witness betwixt me and thee'; the two stipulations, that Jacob not 'afflict' Rachel and Leah by ill-usage, cruelty, or neglect, and that he not 'take other wives beside' them to their grief and dishonour; the appeal to the divine witness, that 'no man is with us' to see how Jacob treats his wives in the privacy of his home, but 'God is witness,' the all-seeing One who beholds what no man can; this being the one point in Laban that shows a father's true care, guarding his daughters' welfare by binding Jacob before God, so the covenant binds Jacob to faithful, kind treatment of his wives under the eye of God).

themes: If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives beside my daughters
no man is with us; see, God is witness betwixt me and thee
Laban's fatherly charge guarding the daughters
sealed by the appeal to God who sees what no man can

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: this pillar, not cast but set up, was certainly set up by Jacob, for in Genesis 31:45 we read 'And Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar'; Laban speaks of it loosely as the common monument; Gill notes Laban continued speaking to Jacob, pointing to the heap and pillar; John recording Laban bidding Jacob behold the heap and the pillar set between them — the formal pointing to the twin monuments that sealed the covenant (Laban, drawing the covenant to its solemn close, pointing to the visible tokens: 'Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have cast betwixt me and thee'; the two monuments named, 'this heap,' the gathered stones, Galeed, and 'this pillar,' the single standing stone Jacob had set up, both set 'betwixt me and thee' as the standing witnesses of the agreement; Clarke's careful observation that the pillar was in fact set up by Jacob, not 'cast' by Laban, so that Laban speaks loosely, claiming a share in the common monument; the heap and pillar together forming the double, enduring witness to which Laban now points as he states the terms of the covenant, so Laban directs Jacob's eye to the standing tokens that would witness the bond between them).

themes: Behold this heap, and behold this pillar
which I have cast betwixt me and thee
Laban pointing to the twin monuments of the covenant
the pillar in fact set up by Jacob, Clarke notes

schools.summary: Gill: Laban, agreeably to the name given the heap by both of them, makes it a witness that neither party would pass over it to the other for harm; John recording Laban declaring the heap and pillar witness that neither he nor Jacob would pass over to the other for harm — the boundary-covenant of mutual non-aggression, sealed by the monuments (Laban stating the heart of the agreement: 'This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness, that I will not pass over this heap to thee, and that thou shalt not pass over this heap and this pillar unto me, for harm'; the substance of the covenant, that the heap and pillar are made the boundary-mark and witness of a mutual pledge of non-aggression, that Laban 'will not pass over' to Jacob's side nor Jacob over to Laban's 'for harm,' with hostile intent; the equal and reciprocal terms, each binding himself not to cross the boundary against the other, so that the monument marks a frontier of peace between the two and their descendants; the covenant, born of distrust, yet securing peace by setting a clear and witnessed limit, so the heap and pillar stand as the witnessed boundary of peace between Jacob and Laban).

themes: This heap be witness, and this pillar be witness
that I will not pass over this heap to thee... for harm
the boundary-covenant of mutual non-aggression
a frontier of peace, born of distrust yet securing peace

schools.summary: Gill: the father of Abraham and Nahor was Terah, so that the god of them is not the true God meant, at least not as truly worshipped, but the gods Terah and Nahor worshipped while idolaters; Jacob does not swear by these but by the fear of his father Isaac, the true and living God; John recording Laban invoking the God of Abraham and of Nahor to judge between them, while Jacob swore by the fear of his father Isaac — the oath that seals the covenant, with Jacob carefully swearing by the true God alone (the covenant sealed with a solemn oath, but the two men not naming God alike: 'The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us. And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac'; the careful distinction, that Laban invokes 'the God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father,' Terah, lumping together the true God and the idols of his idolatrous house, but Jacob, refusing to confound the true God with Laban's gods, 'sware by the fear of his father Isaac' alone, the true and living God whom Isaac reverenced; the debate over Laban's phrase, whether he meant the one true God of the fathers or, in his confusion, the gods of Nahor's idolatry; Jacob's exact piety, swearing only by the true God, so the covenant is sworn, Jacob distinguishing the true God from Laban's idols).

debates.question: Whom does Laban invoke as 'the God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father'?

sides.position: In his confusion Laban lumps the true God with the idols of Terah's and Nahor's idolatrous house; Jacob refuses to swear by these
Jacob swears by 'the fear of his father Isaac' alone — the true and living God — carefully distinguishing him from Laban's gods

themes: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge betwixt us
And Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac
the oath sealing the covenant
Jacob swearing by the true God alone, not Laban's idols

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob offered sacrifice upon mount Gilead — not in a religious way in which he could join Laban or admit him to it, but in a civil way he slew a slaughter, or rather made one, that is, as Jarchi explains, slew cattle for a feast as was usual at the making of covenants; John recording Jacob offering sacrifice on the mount and calling his brethren to eat bread, and they ate and lodged all night — the sacrificial covenant-feast that ratified the peace, kept among Jacob's own (Jacob sealing the covenant with a sacrifice and a feast: 'Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread: and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount'; the acts, that Jacob 'offered sacrifice,' whether a true offering to God or, as Gill suggests, the slaying of cattle for the covenant-feast as was customary at such agreements, and 'called his brethren to eat bread,' the shared covenant-meal that ratified the peace; the careful detail that this was kept among Jacob's 'brethren,' his own kinsmen and company, and that all 'tarried all night in the mount,' lodging together before parting; the feast, with its sacrifice, setting a solemn and sacred seal on the covenant of peace, so the covenant is ratified with sacrifice and the breaking of bread, and the company rests for the night).

themes: Then Jacob offered sacrifice upon the mount, and called his brethren to eat bread
and they did eat bread, and tarried all night in the mount
the sacrificial covenant-feast ratifying the peace
a solemn, sacred seal on the covenant

schools.summary: Gill: early in the morning Laban rose up in order to prepare for and set forward on his journey home, and kissed his sons (his grandsons, Jacob's sons) and his daughters, Rachel and Leah, with Dinah his granddaughter, as was the custom of relations and friends at parting; John recording Laban rising early, kissing and blessing his sons and daughters, then departing and returning to his place — the peaceful parting at last, Laban taking the farewell he had complained of being denied (the long conflict ending in a quiet and kindly parting: 'early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban departed, and returned unto his place'; the change from the hostile pursuer to the affectionate kinsman, that Laban now takes the very leave-taking he had earlier complained of being denied, kissing 'his sons,' his grandsons, and 'his daughters,' Rachel and Leah, and 'blessed them' with a parting blessing; that, the covenant made and the feast shared, Laban 'departed, and returned unto his place,' going home in peace; the providence that brought the dangerous meeting to so peaceful an end, the pursuer who came to harm Jacob now blessing and leaving him, so the whole conflict with Laban closes in peace, and Jacob is free to continue homeward).

themes: And early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them
and Laban departed, and returned unto his place
Laban's peaceful farewell and departure home
the hostile pursuer now blessing and leaving Jacob in peace
